video_title: Strategy Purchases More Bitcoin, Possibility of Rescinding Rule 611 | Bloomberg Crypto 6/16/2026
gist: Bloomberg Crypto (6/16/2026) covers Strategy resuming Bitcoin buys after its first sale since 2022, the SpaceX post-IPO surge as a stress test for crypto-native price discovery of private equity, Citi's tokenized private-share depositary receipts, BlackRock's new covered-call Bitcoin income ETF, the SEC weighing rescinding Rule 611 as the key unlock for tokenized equities, Binance possibly exiting the EU over MiCA licensing, Kalshi's prediction-market records, and the CLARITY Act's path through Congress.

insights.claim: Strategy (Michael Saylor's firm) bought more than 1,500 Bitcoin to raise its reserve to roughly 846,000 tokens, just weeks after disclosing its first Bitcoin sale since 2022, sending a mixed signal to the market.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The symbolic first sale since 2022 contributed to a recent selloff; renewed buying suggests the treasury thesis is intact but the buy/sell cadence is now a sentiment signal to watch.

insights.claim: Analysts expect future Strategy Bitcoin purchases to be funded by credit instruments whose dividends must be paid, which could reduce Bitcoin-per-share going forward.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg reporter
insights.so_what: Declining Bitcoin-per-share would erode the core bull case for owning Strategy stock over spot Bitcoin; track the BTC/share metric each quarter.

insights.claim: Strategy has built a roughly $1.1 billion US dollar reserve specifically to fund dividends on its credit instruments so it can avoid selling Bitcoin during a future downturn, because its core software business does not generate much net income.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg reporter
insights.so_what: The cash reserve is a buffer against forced Bitcoin selling in a drawdown; its size relative to dividend obligations is a key solvency indicator for the leveraged-treasury model.

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO as crypto price-discovery test

insights.claim: SpaceX's post-IPO surge pushed its market value above $2.7 trillion, overtaking Amazon to become roughly the fifth-largest publicly traded company and closing in on Microsoft.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A mega-cap newly public name draws retail and crypto-market speculation; its scale makes it a benchmark for whether on-chain venues can price large equities.

insights.claim: On SpaceX's listing day, over $1 billion traded on its perpetual-swap contract on Hyperliquid, making it the second-biggest perpetuals contract after Bitcoin and signaling real demand for crypto-native exposure to the stock.
insights.speaker: Muyao, Bloomberg crypto reporter
insights.so_what: Crypto perpetuals are becoming a venue for price discovery on hot equities when traditional markets are closed; volume there can foreshadow next-day spot action.

insights.claim: Some crypto exchanges were unable to deliver tokenized SpaceX shares to users on listing Friday, a setback for the tokenization narrative because perpetual swaps are synthetic and need no underlying shares, whereas tokenized stocks require coordination between brokers, crypto platforms, and blockchain settlement.
insights.speaker: Muyao, Bloomberg crypto reporter
insights.so_what: Tokenized-stock delivery failures expose operational risk in the real-asset tokenization model; perpetuals will dominate near-term while spot tokenization plumbing matures.

insights.claim: Citi launched blockchain-enabled depositary receipts allowing trading of tokenized pre-IPO private-company shares, debuting in Zurich in partnership with a Swiss central securities depository, initially only for foreign wealth and institutional investors.
insights.speaker: Shahmir Khaliq, Citi
insights.so_what: A bulge-bracket bank putting private shares into a regulated tokenized wrapper legitimizes the asset class and competes with crypto-native tokenization platforms.

insights.claim: Citi expects a six-to-twelve-month timeline to bring its tokenized private-share product to US institutional and wealth investors, subject to regulatory approval and company-specific disclosure requirements.
insights.speaker: Shahmir Khaliq, Citi
insights.so_what: A concrete US launch window for regulated private-market access; watch for the first non-Citi-portfolio company to onboard as a signal of real demand.

insights.claim: Citi frames tokenized private shares as part of a broader strategy to move payments, liquidity, securities, and collateral on-chain by building blockchain-based rails alongside conventional ones and banking crypto companies directly.
insights.speaker: Shahmir Khaliq, Citi
insights.so_what: Signals Citi is positioning as a full-stack on-chain financial-infrastructure provider, a competitive threat to crypto-native custodians and on/off-ramp businesses.

insights.claim: BlackRock launched a Bitcoin income ETF that uses a covered-call options strategy on its IBIT fund, targeting a 15-20% annual yield while retaining about 70% of Bitcoin's upside.
insights.speaker: Robert (Robbie) Mitchnick, BlackRock head of digital assets
insights.so_what: Gives income-focused advisors and conservative institutions a yield-bearing Bitcoin wrapper; the structure outperforms spot in flat-to-down markets and underperforms in strong rallies.

insights.claim: The BlackRock product monetizes Bitcoin's volatility by selling call options on IBIT, with Mitchnick noting IBIT options are now deeper and more liquid than Bitcoin options themselves.
insights.speaker: Robert (Robbie) Mitchnick, BlackRock head of digital assets
insights.so_what: IBIT options being more liquid than native BTC options shows the ETF has become the dominant institutional options vehicle, a structural shift in where crypto derivatives liquidity sits.

insights.claim: BlackRock says the absence of an intrinsic yield in Bitcoin has been a reason many clients have not allocated, and a yield-plus-70%-upside structure is designed to convert those holdouts.
insights.speaker: Robert (Robbie) Mitchnick, BlackRock head of digital assets
insights.so_what: Identifies yield as the key barrier to incremental institutional Bitcoin allocation; this product could unlock previously sidelined conservative capital.

insights.claim: BlackRock reports Bitcoin allocations are typically funded from a mix of equities, gold, and cash in low-single-digit portfolio weights rather than from large reallocations, and that demand has stayed steady through the cycle despite Bitcoin being down roughly 50% from October all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Robert (Robbie) Mitchnick, BlackRock head of digital assets
insights.so_what: Steady flows in a drawdown suggest sticky institutional demand; the income ETF positions Bitcoin as a yield alternative to non-yielding gold.

insights.claim: The SEC is proposing to rescind Rule 611 (the Order Protection Rule under Reg NMS, in place since 2005), which a Bloomberg Intelligence analyst calls the single biggest obstacle to tokenized equities becoming mainstream.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Intelligence senior crypto analyst
insights.so_what: Rescinding the national-best-bid-and-offer requirement would remove the core legal blocker to tokenized stocks; its repeal is the key regulatory catalyst to watch for the sector.

insights.claim: Beyond Rule 611, tokenized securities face three further obstacles: corporate actions, custody, and securities title, because blockchain settlement collapses intermediaries in ways incompatible with 50-year-old custody rules.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Intelligence senior crypto analyst
insights.so_what: Even if Rule 611 is rescinded, custody and corporate-action plumbing must be modernized before tokenized equities scale; these are the next regulatory bottlenecks.
insights.quote: TECHNOLOGY AS WE'VE SEEN IN SO MANY OTHER ASPECTS OF OUR BUSINESS LIVES IS MOVING MUCH FASTER THAN OUR LAWS CAN ACTUALLY ACCOMMODATE

insights.claim: A Bloomberg Intelligence analyst argues tokenization and 24/7 markets are primarily a response to retail-investor behavior that is already driving US equity volume, crypto, and prediction markets, rather than an attempt to attract retail.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg Intelligence senior crypto analyst
insights.so_what: Reframes market-structure innovation as catch-up to existing retail dominance; the retail flow is the durable driver to track, not the products themselves.

themes.theme: Binance EU exit risk under MiCA

insights.claim: Binance, the world's largest crypto exchange, notified EU customers it may have to exit the region because it has been awaiting a license from Greek regulators and may not obtain one before a July deadline under new rules.
insights.speaker: Anna, Bloomberg senior crypto editor
insights.so_what: A Binance EU withdrawal would be a significant shift for crypto market liquidity and could hand market share to licensed rivals; the two-week licensing deadline is the near-term trigger.

insights.claim: Competing exchanges that secure EU licenses could benefit from Binance's potential exit by absorbing its displaced clients, making MiCA-style licensing a market-share reshuffling event.
insights.speaker: Anna, Bloomberg senior crypto editor
insights.so_what: Watch which exchanges have already obtained EU licenses as potential winners of redistributed Binance volume.

insights.claim: Kalshi set a new weekly record of $6 billion in notional trading volume, powered largely by World Cup betting, with sports accounting for roughly 80% of its volume in the latest count.
insights.speaker: Emily, Bloomberg crypto reporter
insights.so_what: Prediction markets are scaling fast on sports volume; the World Cup is a major near-term catalyst, and the 80% sports mix shows the business is effectively regulated sports betting.

insights.claim: Kalshi cofounder and COO Luana Lopes Lara is worth about $2.6 billion after recent fundraising, and Kalshi and Polymarket are the front-runners in prediction markets ahead of sportsbooks and entrants like Robinhood.
insights.speaker: Emily, Bloomberg crypto reporter
insights.so_what: Maps the competitive landscape: Kalshi and Polymarket lead, with sportsbooks and Robinhood as challengers entering the space.

insights.claim: Kalshi deployed an internal AI agent that continuously scans news and social media to flag when prediction-market events have resolved and to backstop human resolution by checking that two reviewers' answers match.
insights.speaker: Emily, Bloomberg crypto reporter
insights.so_what: Resolution disputes are a known risk that can leave traders unpaid; AI-assisted resolution is an operational moat and a template for the sector.

themes.theme: US crypto legislation and rates backdrop

insights.claim: A Bloomberg Intelligence analyst puts roughly a 70% chance on the CLARITY Act passing, likely in mid-July rather than before July 4, with sticking points around ethics provisions and whether crypto platforms must operate under bank-like AML systems.
insights.speaker: Nathan Dean, Bloomberg Intelligence
insights.so_what: Passage would give crypto platforms regulatory clarity and reduce enforcement risk; the mid-July window and AML/ethics provisions are the key items to monitor.

insights.claim: Crypto platforms are pushing for the CLARITY Act because the analyst suggests they would not be able to operate past 2028 without legislative clarity, while regulators are separately finalizing GENIUS Act stablecoin yield restrictions by year-end that the industry opposes.
insights.speaker: Nathan Dean, Bloomberg Intelligence
insights.so_what: Stablecoin yield restrictions finalizing by year-end are a regulatory headwind for the stablecoin business model; CLARITY passage is existential for platform operations.

insights.claim: Bitcoin rose more than 5% to a two-week high on Monday after an agreement to end hostilities and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, while Brent crude slipped below $80 a barrel for the first time in over three months, yet Bitcoin remains down close to 50% from its October all-time highs.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Geopolitical de-escalation lifted risk assets and pressured oil; Bitcoin's failure to fully participate in the rally despite the relief shows it is still in a deep drawdown.

insights.claim: Robinhood is cutting its workforce by about 10%, eliminating a couple hundred full-time corporate roles, framing it as streamlining for a high-performance culture and earnings growth rather than AI replacement, unlike other fintechs.
insights.speaker: guest, Bloomberg reporter
insights.so_what: Analysts read the cuts as margin-positive for future earnings; the explicit rejection of the AI-layoff narrative differentiates Robinhood's cost story from peers.
